The Queen of Sheba arrives not as a distant observer but as a skeptic who has heard the rumors about Solomon's wisdom and wants to verify them for herself. Yet what she encounters transcends explanation—it must be witnessed. When she sees the palace, the order of his servants, the abundance of his table, and most significantly, his burnt offerings at the Lord's house, she is undone. She came to test Solomon with difficult questions, but what she finds is not merely intellectual superiority; it is the tangible evidence of God's favor. Her confession in verse 9 is the turning point: she recognizes that Solomon's greatness is not his own achievement but the result of the Lord's eternal love for Israel and His delight in placing this king on the throne to execute justice and righteousness. The Queen of Sheba's testimony matters because she comes from outside Israel, and her acknowledgment that the reality exceeds the report validates what God has actually done through Solomon's reign.

Yet as the chapter unfolds, something troubling emerges beneath the glittering surface. The endless enumeration of gold, shields, the throne with its twelve lions, the ships arriving every three years—these details accumulate into something that feels less like blessing and more like intoxication with wealth and power. Verse 21 is particularly striking: silver becomes worthless, accounted as nothing. Solomon has become so rich that the precious has become common. The text records what should astonish us, and it does, but not always in the way we might wish. God gave Solomon wisdom to lead, yet here we see a king whose attention has increasingly turned to accumulation, display, and the fascination of tribute flowing in from every direction.

Most disturbing is what remains unspoken: the text makes no mention of Solomon's obedience to the specific command God gave him in chapter 3, where the Lord warned him not to multiply horses or return to Egypt for them. Yet verses 26-29 detail exactly this—1,400 chariots, 12,000 horses imported from Egypt and Kue, sold to other kings for profit. This is not peripheral detail. Solomon is violating the very limitations God placed upon him as conditions for his continued blessing. The chapter presents his magnificence as if it were unambiguous good, yet Deuteronomy 17:16 explicitly forbids the king of Israel from multiplying horses or sending people back to Egypt. What we read as the summit of Solomon's wisdom and success contains within it the seeds of his future rebellion and the nation's eventual fracture.

We must not rush past this tension. The Queen of Sheba rightly sees that God has set Solomon on the throne to execute justice and righteousness—this is true. But a king who accumulates beyond what God has authorized, who contradicts the explicit boundaries placed upon his office, who allows the precious to become common through endless acquisition, has begun to drift from that calling. His wisdom remains genuine, his wealth is real, his fame is well-founded—and yet none of this protects him from the subtle corruption of believing that more is always better, that the constraints God placed upon him do not truly apply. This is the danger of unchecked blessing: it can blind us to our own disobedience.
